title: Reading is a System
subtitle: Improve your reading system for the rest of your life by utilizing these strategies

strategies.title: Parallel Reading
strategies.subtitle: Read several sources at once to expose shared claims, tensions, and gaps.
strategies.body: Keep two to five texts active around the same problem. Move between them deliberately: when one author makes a claim, look for confirmation, disagreement, or missing context in the others. The goal is not finishing faster; it is building a stronger map of the field.

strategies.title: Discursive Reading
strategies.subtitle: Let a source send you sideways through references, names, and arguments.
strategies.body: Treat reading as a conversation with the surrounding material. Follow footnotes, unfamiliar entities, cited papers, and recurring terms. Capture why each detour mattered so the path becomes a useful trail rather than a pile of tabs.

strategies.title: Field Notes
strategies.subtitle: Capture observations close to the moment before they harden into summaries.
strategies.body: Write short, dated notes while reading, watching, talking, or testing. Keep them concrete: what happened, what changed, what surprised you, and what you might inspect next. Later synthesis gets better when the raw observations stay intact.

strategies.title: Entity Shorthand
strategies.subtitle: Use compact labels for people, places, works, institutions, and concepts.
strategies.body: Give recurring entities stable shorthand so your notes stay fast without becoming vague. A person, book, theory, institution, project, or place can each get a memorable token. The shorthand becomes useful when it is consistent enough to search later.

strategies.title: YouTube Playlists
strategies.subtitle: Turn watch queues into intentional research sequences.
strategies.body: Build playlists around questions instead of channels. Sort videos by what they add: overview, case study, dissent, tutorial, interview, or primary source. Add a sentence after watching so the playlist becomes a trail of judgments, not just saved media.

strategies.title: Internet Worldbuilding
strategies.subtitle: Study online spaces as systems with norms, artifacts, and histories.
strategies.body: When entering a forum, scene, tool ecosystem, or platform, identify the local vocabulary, status signals, recurring disputes, origin stories, and canonical artifacts. The internet becomes more legible when each space is treated as a world with rules.

strategies.title: Exponential Dropoff
strategies.subtitle: Expect attention, quality, and relevance to decay unless the system filters hard.
strategies.body: Most queues decay quickly: links age, tabs lose context, and saved items stop mattering. Design for dropoff by reviewing aggressively, deleting without ceremony, and only promoting material that earns a second pass.
